title: Storage, Filesystems & Disk Management
description: Storage on Linux spans a hierarchy: physical disks → partitions → LVM (logical volumes) → filesystems → mount points. At Enterprise, storage is abstracted through multiple layers: local NVMe SSD, EBS volumes, network filesystems (NFS/EFS), and distributed filesystems (Ceph, JuiceFS). Understanding the Linux storage stack is essential for capacity planning, performance tuning, and data integrity.

sections.title: Disk Discovery & Partitioning
sections.description: The first step in storage management is discovering what disks are available. Modern Linux uses lsblk and fdisk/parted for partitioning. At Enterprise, disks are typically provisioned via cloud APIs (EBS volumes, persistent disks) and formatted automatically by init scripts — manual partitioning is rare but understanding it is expected for system-level roles.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Disk discovery ===
lsblk                                # Tree view of all block devices
lsblk -f                             # Include filesystem info (FSTYPE, UUID, LABEL)
lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT,FSTYPE,UUID  # Custom columns

sudo fdisk -l                        # Detailed partition table
sudo parted -l                       # Alternative partition tool

# Device naming:
# /dev/sda, /dev/sdb, ...  — SCSI/SATA disks (also virtio-blk, EBS)
# /dev/nvme0n1, /dev/nvme1n1, ... — NVMe SSDs
# /dev/vda, /dev/vdb, ...  — VirtIO disks (KVM)
# /dev/mapper/*            — LVM logical volumes

# === Partitioning with parted (GPT) ===
sudo parted /dev/sdb mklabel gpt                      # Create GPT label
sudo parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary ext4 0% 100%      # Use entire disk
sudo parted /dev/sdb mkpart primary ext4 1MiB 10GiB   # Specific size
sudo parted /dev/sdb print                            # Show partition table

# === Partitioning with fdisk (MBR) ===
sudo fdisk /dev/sdb                                   # Interactive
# n → new partition, p → primary, w → write

# === Partition tables: MBR vs GPT ===
# MBR (DOS): max 2TB, 4 primary partitions
# GPT:       up to 8ZiB, 128 partitions, redundant header
# Most modern systems use GPT

# === Wipe a disk ===
sudo wipefs -a /dev/sdb              # Wipe all filesystem signatures
sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sdb bs=1M count=100  # Overwrite first 100MB

# === Kernel re-scan ===
sudo partprobe                        # Inform kernel of partition table changes
echo 1 | sudo tee /sys/block/sdb/device/rescan

sections.title: Filesystems: ext4, XFS & Modern Options
sections.description: The filesystem is the layer that organizes data on disk. ext4 is the default on Ubuntu/Debian — reliable and well-tested. XFS is the default on RHEL/Amazon Linux — excellent for large files and parallel I/O. At Enterprise, the choice of filesystem depends on workload: XFS for large distributed databases, ext4 for general-purpose, and specialised FS for specific use cases (ZFS for data integrity, Btrfs for snapshots).
sections.codeBlock:
# === Creating filesystems ===
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1              # Format as ext4
sudo mkfs.ext4 -L mydata /dev/sdb1    # With label
sudo mkfs.xfs /dev/sdb1               # Format as XFS
sudo mkfs.xfs -L mydata /dev/sdb1     # XFS with label
sudo mkfs.btrfs /dev/sdb1             # Btrfs

# === ext4 tuning ===
sudo mkfs.ext4 -m 0 /dev/sdb1         # 0% reserved for root (was 5%, good for data volumes)
sudo mkfs.ext4 -E lazy_itable_init=0 /dev/sdb1  # Initialize inode table (faster first mount? Actually slower now, faster later)
# tune2fs: tune an existing ext2/3/4 filesystem
sudo tune2fs -l /dev/sdb1             # List filesystem features
sudo tune2fs -m 1 /dev/sdb1           # Change reserved block percentage

# === XFS tuning ===
# XFS is optimized for large files and parallel I/O
# Key mkfs.xfs options:
# -d agcount=4     — Allocation group count (more = better parallelism)
# -f               — Force (overwrite existing FS)
# -m crc=1         — Enable metadata CRC (default since RHEL 7)

# === Filesystem features comparison ===
# Feature           ext4            XFS             Btrfs
# Max file size     16TB            8EB             16EB
# Max volume size   1EB             8EB             16EB
# Default fsck      e2fsck          xfs_repair      btrfs check
# Snapshots         no              no              yes
# Compression       no              no              yes
# Online defrag     no              yes (xfs_fsr)   yes

# === Check filesystem ===
sudo fsck /dev/sdb1                  # Check and repair (must be unmounted!)
sudo fsck.ext4 -f /dev/sdb1          # Force check even if clean
sudo xfs_repair /dev/sdb1            # XFS repair (unmounted)
sudo xfs_check /dev/sdb1             # XFS check (unmounted)

sections.title: Mounting & /etc/fstab
sections.description: Mounting attaches a filesystem to a directory. The /etc/fstab file defines persistent mounts that are automatically mounted at boot. A misconfigured fstab can prevent a server from booting — always test mount options and keep a backup.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Mount basics ===
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data       # Mount device to directory
sudo umount /mnt/data                # Unmount
sudo mount -a                        # Mount everything in /etc/fstab

# === /etc/fstab format ===
# <device>    <mountpoint>    <fstype>    <options>    <dump>    <pass>

# Examples:
UUID=abc123-...  /                 ext4    defaults,errors=remount-ro  0       1
UUID=def456-...  /boot             ext4    defaults                   0       2
UUID=ghi789-...  /mnt/data        ext4    defaults,noatime           0       2

# /dev/sdb1      /mnt/data        ext4    defaults,nofail             0       2
# ^ nofail means: if this fails to mount, still boot

# === Common mount options ===
# defaults       — rw, suid, dev, exec, auto, nouser, async
# noatime        — Don't update access time (big performance gain)
# nodiratime     — Don't update directory access times
# noexec         — Don't allow executables on this filesystem
# nosuid         — Ignore SUID bits
# ro             — Read-only
# nofail         — Don't fail boot if mount fails (important for removable/optional volumes)
# discard        — Enable TRIM for SSDs (or better: periodic fstrim)

# === Bind mounts ===
# Mount a directory to another location
mount --bind /var/log /mnt/log-backup
# /etc/fstab:
# /var/log  /mnt/log-backup  none   bind   0   0

# === tmpfs (RAM-backed filesystem) ===
mount -t tmpfs -o size=1G tmpfs /mnt/ramdisk
# /etc/fstab:
# tmpfs  /mnt/ramdisk  tmpfs  size=1G  0  0

# === Find by UUID ===
blkid                                # List all UUIDs and LABELs
sudo mount UUID="abc123" /mnt/data   # Mount by UUID (preferred, immune to device name changes)

# === Check mounts ===
mount                                # List all mounted filesystems
df -hT                               # Disk free, human-readable + type
findmnt                              # Tree view of mounts

sections.title: LVM: Logical Volume Manager
sections.description: LVM provides a flexible abstraction layer between physical disks and filesystems. With LVM, you can resize volumes without repartitioning, take snapshots for backups, and span volumes across multiple disks. At Enterprise, LVM is used on on-premise servers but cloud instances typically use cloud block storage directly (EBS volumes) — though understanding LVM is still expected for infrastructure roles.
sections.codeBlock:
# === LVM Architecture ===
# Physical Volumes (PVs) → Volume Group (VG) → Logical Volumes (LVs)

# === Create LVM ===
# 1. Mark disk as Physical Volume
sudo pvcreate /dev/sdb /dev/sdc

# 2. Create Volume Group
sudo vgcreate vg_data /dev/sdb /dev/sdc

# 3. Create Logical Volume
sudo lvcreate -L 100G -n lv_data vg_data
sudo lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n lv_data vg_data     # Use all free space

# 4. Format and mount
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg_data/lv_data
sudo mkdir /mnt/data
sudo mount /dev/vg_data/lv_data /mnt/data

# === LVM commands ===
pvs                                    # List physical volumes
pvs -o+pv_used                         # With usage info
vgs                                    # List volume groups
vgs -o+vg_free                         # With free space
lvs                                    # List logical volumes
lvdisplay                              # Detailed LV info

# === Resize LVM ===
# Extend PV (if underlying disk grew)
sudo pvresize /dev/sdb

# Extend VG (add new disk)
sudo vgextend vg_data /dev/sdd

# Extend LV (then resize filesystem)
sudo lvextend -L +50G /dev/vg_data/lv_data
sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/vg_data/lv_data

# Resize filesystem (ext4)
sudo resize2fs /dev/vg_data/lv_data

# Resize filesystem (xfs)
sudo xfs_growfs /mnt/data               # XFS grows online (must be mounted)

# === LVM Snapshots ===
# Create a snapshot (for backup consistency)
sudo lvcreate -L 10G -s -n lv_data_snap /dev/vg_data/lv_data
# Mount snapshot read-only:
sudo mount -o ro /dev/vg_data/lv_data_snap /mnt/backup
# Remove snapshot:
sudo lvremove /dev/vg_data/lv_data_snap

# === Remove LVM ===
sudo umount /mnt/data
sudo lvremove /dev/vg_data/lv_data
sudo vgremove vg_data
sudo pvremove /dev/sdb /dev/sdc

sections.title: Monitoring: I/O, Space & Inodes
sections.description: Storage monitoring is critical for production reliability. Out of disk space is the #1 cause of predictable production incidents — it's almost always a log file or database that wasn't rotated. At Enterprise, disk monitoring is built into the observability stack, but you still need to know the command-line tools for ad-hoc debugging.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Disk space ===
df -h                                # Human-readable disk usage
df -hT                               # With filesystem type
df -i                                # Inode usage (filesystem can be full even with free space!)

# === Directory size ===
du -sh /var/log                      # Total size of directory
du -sh /*                            # Size of top-level directories
du -sh /var/log/* | sort -rh | head -10  # Largest items in /var/log

# === I/O performance ===
iostat -x 1                          # I/O stats by device (1s intervals)
# Key fields:
# %util      — % of time device was busy (warning at >80%)
# await      — Average I/O latency (ms)
# r/s, w/s   — Read/write IOPS
# rkB/s, wkB/s — Throughput

iostat -x 1 5                        # 5 samples, 1 second apart

# === I/O top (per-process) ===
iotop -oP                            # Show only processes doing I/O

# === Find large files ===
find /var/log -type f -size +100M    # Files > 100MB
find / -xdev -type f -size +1G -exec ls -lh {} \;  # Files > 1GB on root FS

# === Find and remove old logs ===
find /var/log -name "*.log" -mtime +30 -delete  # Delete logs > 30 days

# === TRIM for SSDs ===
sudo fstrim -v /                     # Manually TRIM a filesystem
sudo fstrim -av                      # TRIM all mounted FS that support it
# Systemd timer: systemctl status fstrim.timer

# === SMART disk health ===
sudo apt install smartmontools
sudo smartctl -H /dev/sda            # Overall health status
sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda            # Full SMART data
sudo smartctl -t short /dev/sda      # Run short self-test

# === dd benchmark ===
dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/test bs=1M count=1000 oflag=direct  # Write test
dd if=/tmp/test of=/dev/null bs=1M count=1000 iflag=direct   # Read test
# Replace with fio for production-grade benchmarks

# === fio (Flexible I/O Tester) ===
sudo apt install fio
fio --randrepeat=1 --ioengine=libaio --direct=1 --gtod_reduce=1 \
   --name=test --bs=4k --iodepth=64 --size=1G --readwrite=randread \
   --filename=/tmp/fio-test

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'The database server has no space, but df shows 50% free. What's wrong?'

A: 'Check df -i — the filesystem may be out of inodes, not space. This happens when millions of tiny files (like Docker overlay layers, mail spool files, or a runaway process writing temp files) exhaust the inode table. Inode count is set at filesystem creation and can't be changed without reformatting. The fix: find and remove the unnecessary small files, or create a new filesystem with more inodes using mkfs.ext4 -N <count>.'

Q: 'How do you choose between ext4 and XFS for a new filesystem?'

A: 'It depends on the workload. For general-purpose, ext4 is fine. For large files (>2TB), high concurrency of parallel I/O, or workloads with many large files (media, logs, databases), XFS performs better — especially because it supports online defragmentation (xfs_fsr) and has better allocation groups for parallelism. For metadata-heavy workloads (many small files, like a mail server or git repository), ext4 is typically better. At Enterprise, the standard is often XFS on RHEL/Amazon Linux for production and ext4 on Ubuntu for development.'

Q: 'You receive an alert: EBS volume is at 95% capacity. Walk through your response.'

A: 'First, acknowledge the alert. SSH into the instance (or use session manager). Run df -h to confirm which volume. Run du -sh /* | sort -rh | head -10 to find the largest directories. Identify the culprit (logs, database WAL files, temp files). If it's logs: rotate/compress with logrotate or delete old logs. If it's predictable growth, extend the EBS volume (increase size in cloud console), then sudo growpart /dev/xvda 1 (extend partition) and sudo resize2fs /dev/xvda1 (extend filesystem). File a ticket to increase monitoring thresholds or add auto-scaling. Document the incident.'

sections.description:
• Arch Linux Filesystems Wiki — Comprehensive filesystem comparison
  • LVM Administration (RHEL 9) — Red Hat's official LVM guide
  • mkfs.ext4(8) man page — All ext4 formatting options

learningObjectives: Understand filesystems: ext4, XFS & Modern Options

analogy.title: Storage as a Filing Cabinet
analogy.description: Block devices are empty drawers. Filesystems (ext4, XFS) are the filing system - they organize how papers are stored. LVM adds flexible drawers that can grow. Mount points are labels on the drawer fronts telling you what is inside. RAID is keeping duplicate copies in multiple cabinets.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Disk Discovery & Partitioning
practiceLabs.task: Run `lsblk` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man lsblk` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Filesystems: ext4, XFS & Modern Options
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Mounting & /etc/fstab
practiceLabs.task: Run `echo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man echo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `lsblk` | Disk Discovery & Partitioning |
| `sudo` | Disk Discovery & Partitioning |
| `echo` | Disk Discovery & Partitioning |
| `sudo` | Filesystems: ext4, XFS & Modern Options |
| `sudo` | Mounting & /etc/fstab |
| `UUID` | Mounting & /etc/fstab |
| `mount` | Mounting & /etc/fstab |
| `sudo` | LVM: Logical Volume Manager |
| `pvs` | LVM: Logical Volume Manager |
| `vgs` | LVM: Logical Volume Manager |
| `df` | Monitoring: I/O, Space & Inodes |
| `du` | Monitoring: I/O, Space & Inodes |
| `iostat` | Monitoring: I/O, Space & Inodes |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man lsblk`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: storage operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Disk Discovery & Partitioning?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Disk Discovery & Partitioning is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `lsblk` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the storage directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a storage directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a storage scenario, your monitoring alerts show lsblk failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Storage management has changed dramatically with cloud computing
FAANG, you rarely touch physical drives — you provision EBS volumes, create snapshots, and manage lifecycle policies through APIs
productionTips: Production Tip:  Set up monitoring on disk inode usage, not just space usage
production outages caused by 'disk space OK but inodes full' — Docker overlay filesystems and log rotation failures are common culprits
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, storage, filesystems & disk management is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
